verses.commentary: Jesus returns to 'his own city' — Capernaum, his ministry base. Galilean lake-traversing punctuates the narrative.
Paralytic carried on a mat. 'Their faith' — including the bearers' faith. 'Take heart, son' — pastoral warmth. 'Your sins are forgiven' — surprising opening. The disease is not the deepest problem.
Scribes silently object: 'blaspheming.' Only God forgives sins. They have correctly identified the implicit claim — but call it blasphemy rather than self-disclosure.
Jesus knows their thoughts — interior reading is a divine attribute (Psalm 139). The accusation is internal; the response is public.
Which is easier — to say 'sins forgiven' or 'rise and walk'? The first is harder to verify but easier to claim. The second is empirically testable.

'That you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins' — the visible miracle authenticates the invisible. Verifiable healing certifies the unverifiable forgiveness. Christology embedded in healing.
Man rises and walks home. Carrying the mat that carried him. The reversal is visual.
Crowd glorifies God who 'gave such authority to men' (anthropois, plural). The Son of Man's authority opens authority for the community — anticipating 18:18 and 28:18.
Matthew at the tax-collection booth (telonion, τελώνιον). 'Follow me' — terse imperative. Matthew rises and follows. The Gospel's author identifies himself by occupation — a collaborator with Rome.
Dinner at Matthew's house. Tax collectors and sinners recline with Jesus and disciples. Table fellowship signaled solidarity in first-century Judaism — Jesus dissolves the boundary.
Pharisees object via the disciples — indirect challenge. They cannot fathom holiness eating with impurity. Their theology cannot account for this Messiah.
Doctor analogy: the healthy don't need a physician. Jesus' mission requires proximity to disease, not distance from it. Ministry is not a quarantine.
'Mercy not sacrifice' — Hosea 6:6 quoted (and again at 12:7). Mercy outranks ritual. 'Not to call the righteous but sinners' — sarcastic edge: the self-described righteous exclude themselves.
John's disciples plus Pharisees fast; Jesus' do not. A pious gap noticed. The question is genuine, not hostile.

Bridegroom imagery — Jesus is the bridegroom (compare Isaiah 62:5; Matthew 25:1-13). Guests at a wedding do not fast. But the bridegroom will be taken away (first hint at the cross) — then they will fast.
Unshrunk patch on old garment — the new shrinks and tears the old. Continuity-with-rupture: Jesus' newness cannot be sewn onto the old religious system without damage.
New wine in old wineskins — bursts. New wine, new wineskins. The gospel requires new forms; old containers cannot hold the new ferment. Kingdom theology breaks legacy structures.
Synagogue ruler (later named Jairus in Mark) — religious establishment figure pleading for daughter's life. Faith from unexpected place. 'Lay your hand on her, and she will live.'
Jesus rises and follows. The Messiah goes when called — accessible, not aloof.
Twelve years bleeding — chronic, ritually defiling (Leviticus 15). She touches the fringe (kraspedon, κράσπεδον, tzitzit, prayer-tassel — Numbers 15:38). Reaches the holy without permission.
Interior monologue — 'if I only touch.' Faith mixed with semi-magical thinking. Yet faith it is. Jesus honors imperfect faith.
Jesus turns and sees. 'Daughter' — kinship address. 'Your faith has saved you' (sesoken, σέσωκέν — same root as salvation). The healing is named theologically: salvation in microcosm.
Mourners and flute players — first-century funeral was loud and public. Hired professional grief. The death is being institutionalized when Jesus arrives.
'Not dead but sleeping' — euphemism that anticipates resurrection theology (compare John 11:11). They laugh at him — the mockery foreshadows the cross.
Crowd dismissed, room cleared. He takes her hand — touching a corpse, again contaminating cleanness in reverse. She rises.
News spreads through the region. The miracle resists suppression; the messianic identity leaks.
Two blind men (Matthew's pattern of doubling). 'Son of David' — first use in Matthew as a confessional title in plain speech. Even blind men see what scribes do not.
'Do you believe?' — faith is the explicit prerequisite. Jesus probes before performing.
'According to your faith let it be done.' Faith calibrates the miracle's reception, not Jesus' power. The grant matches the trust.
Sight given. Sternly warned (enebrimethe, ἐνεβριμήθη — strong warning) not to tell. Messianic secret — Jesus restrains premature acclamation that would derail his mission.
They tell anyway. Restored sight, disobedient testimony. Jesus' restraint loses to their enthusiasm.
Demon-possessed mute brought. The disability and the possession are linked. Each healing in this chapter restores a different lost faculty.
Demon out, voice restored. The crowd: 'Nothing like this has ever been seen in Israel.' Acknowledgment of unprecedented era — though they still do not name him Messiah.
Pharisees' counter-narrative: he casts out demons by the prince of demons. The same facts framed two ways. The miracles do not coerce belief — interpretation is the battleground.
Summary statement: teaching, proclaiming, healing — through every town and village. The threefold mission of Jesus that the Twelve will inherit in chapter 10.

Compassion (esplagchnisthe, ἐσπλαγχνίσθη — gut-level mercy). Crowds 'harassed and helpless' — sheep without shepherd (Numbers 27:17; Ezekiel 34). Israel's leaders have failed; Jesus is the true shepherd.
Harvest plentiful, workers few. The need exceeds available laborers. Mission scaling problem stated theologically.
'Ask the Lord of the harvest' — prayer comes before recruitment. The next chapter answers the prayer: twelve sent. Prayer and sending are sequential, not alternative.
